what:
OpenFn adaptor for accessing objects in Asana. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to Asana REST API.

It exposes 8 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. 15 ready-to-paste snippets are grouped as create (9) and search (6). Vendor homepage: https://asana.com.
connect:
Asana supports OAuth 2.0 or bearer tokens for API access.

The credential schema asks for apiVersion, token (required), and workspaceGid (required). Point the credential at the base URL: https://app.asana.com/api/1.0. Auth reference: https://developers.asana.com/docs/authentication.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in Asana REST API.

12 triggers are catalogued (3 webhook, 9 polling), including Completed Task, New Comment on a Task, New Completed Subtask, New Project, and more. See the Triggers tab for the full list. Asana can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://developers.asana.com/docs/webhooks), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Kick the workflow off from a trigger on the Triggers tab (webhook-driven where supported, polling otherwise). Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in Asana. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running Asana jobs at volume:

- Prefer Asana webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Paginate list and search calls explicitly; never assume a single response is the full result set.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.
best:
- Store Asana credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-asana to v5.0.12 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Validate inbound trigger payloads against the schemas on the Data tab before transforming them; payloads can drift between vendor versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
